coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-induced preparation of novel CNTs/PbBiO2Cl nanosheet photocatalyst with boosted photocatalytic activity for the removal of organic contaminants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zihan
Yin, Sheng
Hu, Qingsong
Ding, Yi
Di, Jun
Xia, Jiexiang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: Novel layered PbBiO2Cl material exhibited great application potential in the fields of photocatalytic energy conversion and environmental remediation. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic efficiency of pure PbBiO2Cl material is still far from the requirements of practical application due to insufficient light capture ability and rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers. Herein, carbon nanotubes (CNTs)/PbBiO2Cl nanosheets have been synthesized via the ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium chlorine ([C16mim]Cl) assisted hydrothermal method. The [C16mim]Cl anchored more CNTs on the surface of PbBiO2Cl material, enabling intimately integration of CNTs and PbBiO2Cl. The rhodamine B (RhB), tetracycline and ciprofloxacin were selected as the target pollutants to evaluate the photocatalytic pollutants degradation performance of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites under visible light irradiation. The 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl exhibited the highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency (92.5%) toward RhB under visible light irradiation for 2 h. The results of steady-state fluorescence and photoelectrochemical characterizations demonstrated that the intimate structure between CNTs and PbBiO2Cl was favorable for the transfer and separation of photogenerated carriers. This study presents a new insight into enhance molecular oxygen activation for photocatalytic degradation organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquid
PbBiO2Cl nanosheets
CNTs
Photodegradation
Visible light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photocatalytic degradation efficiency curves of RhB and (b) the corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of RhB over pure PbBiO2Cl and different CNTs/PbBiO2Cl samples under visible light irradiation for 2 h.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation efficiency curves of TC and (b) the corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of TC over pure PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl sample under visible light irradiation for 6 h; (c) Photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CIP and (d) the corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of CIP over pure PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl sample under visible light irradiation for 4 h.
XRD patterns of CNTs, PbBiO2Cl and CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites with different CNTs contents (1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt%, 8 wt%).
TEM images of (a) pure PbBiO2Cl and (b) 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl; (c) HRTEM images of pure PbBiO2Cl.
XPS spectra of pure PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl: (a) Survey, (b) Pb 4f, (c) Bi 4f, (d) O 1 s, (e) Cl 2p, (f) C 1 s.
Nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms of (a) pure PbBiO2Cl and (b) 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of different samples, (b) PL spectra, (c) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, (d) transient photocurrent response of PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl.

Radicals quenching experiment of active species during the degradation of RhB over 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl under visible light irradiation. (each set of photocatalytic activity was repeated for three times).

ESR spectra of radical adducts trapped by DMPO (O2 • − and •OH) in PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl in the dark and under visible light irradiation: (a) in methanol dispersion for DMPO-O2 • −, (b) in aqueous dispersion for DMPO-•OH.

Schematic diagram illustrating the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites under visible light irradiation of along with the possible photocatalytic mechanism.
Schematic illustration of the formation process of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-induced preparation of novel CNTs/PbBiO2Cl nanosheet photocatalyst with boosted photocatalytic activity for the removal of organic contaminants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore, 639798, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel layered PbBiO2Cl material exhibited great application potential in the fields of photocatalytic energy conversion and environmental remediation. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic efficiency of pure PbBiO2Cl material is still far from the requirements of practical application due to insufficient light capture ability and rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers. Herein, carbon nanotubes (CNTs)/PbBiO2Cl nanosheets have been synthesized via the ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium chlorine ([C16mim]Cl) assisted hydrothermal method. The [C16mim]Cl anchored more CNTs on the surface of PbBiO2Cl material, enabling intimately integration of CNTs and PbBiO2Cl. The rhodamine B (RhB), tetracycline and ciprofloxacin were selected as the target pollutants to evaluate the photocatalytic pollutants degradation performance of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites under visible light irradiation. The 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl exhibited the highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency (92.5%) toward RhB under visible light irradiation for 2 h. The results of steady-state fluorescence and photoelectrochemical characterizations demonstrated that the intimate structure between CNTs and PbBiO2Cl was favorable for the transfer and separation of photogenerated carriers. This study presents a new insight into enhance molecular oxygen activation for photocatalytic degradation organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel sewage disposal technology, semiconductor photocatalysis has drawn widespread concern owing to the convenient operation, high efficiency and free secondary pollution [1–4] . Conventional photocatalysts, such as TiO2, has been generally studied in the field of photocatalysis due to stability and hypotoxicity. However, TiO2 merely responds to ultraviolet light (about 4% in sunlight), which could not fully utilize solar energy [5]. Consequently, it is of great importance for broadening the light response range, thus boosting the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts [6–8] .

Layered PbBiO2X (X = Cl, Br, I) material with unique layered structure and stable chemical properties have attracted extensive research interest [9,10]. PbBiO2X adopts a Sillén structure with fluorite-type PbBiO2 layers intervened by halide anions [11]. Among them, PbBiO2Cl has been widely investigated since the controllable morphology, suitable bandgap and high stability [12]. At present, the synthesis methods of PbBiO2Cl photocatalysts mainly focus on solid state reaction, hydrothermal method and ionic liquid (IL) assisted solvothermal route. Huang’s group reported that layered PbBiO2Cl material was prepared via calcination at 700 °C for 10 h in air, which displayed good photodegrade activity. Under visible light irradiation for 10 h, the methyl orange degradation efficiency of PbBiO2Cl photocatalyst reached 89% [13]. Cao’s group synthesized PbBiO2Cl nanostructure by simple hydrothermal method using BiCl3 as the Cl source. 45% 4-chlorophenol can be degrade by PbBiO2Cl material after visible light irradiation for 480 min, which was about 5 times higher than nitrogen-modified TiO2 (8.7%) [14]. However, the morphology of PbBiO2Cl material is uncontrolled. Meanwhile, smaller specific surface area made fewer active sites exposed, thus limiting the photocatalytic degradation performance. Consequently, it’s necessary to further explore the synthesis method of PbBiO2Cl photocatalyst.

The latest research suggested that IL plays a significant role in the synthesis of various inorganic multifunctional materials, considering different characteristics such as reactant, solvent, dispersing agent, template, and so on [15,16]. Yin’s group fabricated g-C3N4/PbBiO2Cl composite materials via IL 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium chlorine ([C16mim]Cl)-assisted solvothermal route. [C16mim]Cl not only provides Cl source, but also was used to be template agent to induce the formation of PbBiO2Cl porous microspheres. The as-prepared g-C3N4/PbBiO2Cl composites exhibited enhanced photocatalytic degradation activity over rhodamine B (RhB), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and bisphenol A [17]. Among them, under light irradiation for 100 min, 3 wt% g-C3N4/PbBiO2Cl composite displayed the highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency over RhB reached 96%. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of pure PbBiO2Cl for industrial application is restricted by deficient light absorption and high recombination of photogenerated carriers. Accordingly, a sequence of strategies have been developed to boost the photocatalytic activity of PbBiO2Cl material, such as morphological control [18], doping [17] and coupling of semiconductors [19,20]. Among which the coupling of semiconductors strategy has been proved to be able to enhance the photocatalytic performance of PbBiO2Cl. In comparision to single PbBiO2Cl, PbBiO2Cl-based composite catalysts display enhanced photocatalytic activities, such as, CQDs/PbBiO2Cl [17], MoS2/PbBiO2Cl [21], g-C3N4/PbBiO2Cl [22] and so on. These research results indicated that the effective separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs played an important role in improving the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts. Therefore, choosing a suitable electron acceptor would accelerate the migration of photogenerated carriers, thus improving photocatalytic efficiency.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) has been demonstrated to generate benefical effects on the catalytic performance of catalysts, given that its excellent electron transfer ability. It has been widely applied in the fields of environmental remediation, clean energy and organic synthesis [23–25]. Various photocatalytic systems based on CNTs were explored and the enhanced photocatalytic activity have been obtained, such as g-C3N4 [26], ZnO [27], CdS [28] and Bi2WO6 [29]. Nonetheless, the interaction between CNTs and semiconductor materials needs further research in particular. Hence, the hybrid photocatalytic system of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl could be built in terms of the advantage of CNTs and PbBiO2Cl. As far as we know, there are relatively few studies devoted to the CNTs modified PbBiO2Cl photocatalytic system.

In this study, CNTs/PbBiO2Cl hybrid materials were controlled synthesized via the reactive IL assisted solvothermal process. [C16mim]Cl was applied as the source of Cl as well as a bridge to congregate CNTs and PbBiO2Cl. The strong coupling between CNTs and [C16mim]Cl can be constructed based on the coulomb force and hydrogen bond between the two components [30–32]. Subsequently, the chlorine ions in [C16mim]Cl could react with lead nitrate and bismuth nitrate to form PbBiO2Cl and induce the strong interaction between CNTs and PbBiO2Cl. Consequently, the outstanding electron conductivity of CNTs could be fully utilized. The photocatalytic activity of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites were assessed by the degradation of antibiotic agent RhB, tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and CIP under visible light irradiation. The structure-activity relationship between CNTs and PbBiO2Cl had been explored in detail. The possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed in terms of the reactive-species-quenching experiments, electron spin resonance (ESR) technique and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) valence band. This study develops strategy for PbBiO2Cl material further applied in the field of photocatalytic environmental remediation.

All the chemical reagents are of analytical grade. The CNTs (＞95%) was obtained from Chengdu Organic Chemicals Co. Ltd., Chinese Academy of Sciences. [C16mim]Cl (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium chlorine) (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co.Ltd.

Synthesis of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites was shown below ( Scheme 1). First of all, 1 mmol Pb(NO3)2, 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O and a certain amount of CNTs were added into 10 mL deionized water and stirred continuously with 800 rpm until the solution was evenly mixed, and labeled as solution A. Then, 1 mmol [C16mim]Cl was added into 10 mL anhydrous ethanol and ultrasonic dispersed evenly, labeled as solution B. Finally, solution B was added into solution A drop by drop and stirred for 30 min with 800 rpm. After the solution was completely dispersed, it was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After centrifugation, the reaction products were washed with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water for 3 times. The final products were obtained by drying at 50 °C for 24 h. The added contents of CNTs in CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites were 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt%, and 8 wt%, respectively. Single PbBiO2Cl was prepared without adding CNTs.
This part was in the supplementary information.

Firstly, 0.03 g catalysts were added into 100 mL rhodamine B (RhB) (10 mg/L), 100 mL tetracycline (TC) (20 mg/L), and 100 mL ciprofloxacin (CIP) (10 mg/L) solutions, respectively. The distance between the reaction solution and the light source was about 5 cm. Before the photocatalytic reaction, the suspensions were stirred in the darkness for about 30 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The whole process was carried out continuously in a circulating water bath at 30 °C, employing a 300 W Xenon lamp with a 400 nm cutoff filter. During the photocatalytic process, about 4 mL suspension was extracted continuously at set intervals, followed by centrifugation to eliminate the samples. Eventually, the concentrations of RhB, TC, CIP were detected employing a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 553 nm, 356 nm and 276 nm.
This part was in the Supplementary information.

The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared samples were evaluated toward different organic pollutants photodegradation under visible light irradiation. The rhodamine B (RhB) with carcinogenic properties was selected as the target pollutant and the adsorption curves were displayed in Fig. S1a. It can be observed the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was realized between the photocatalysts and pollutants after adsorbing for 30 min in the dark. As shown in Fig. 1 a, after light irradiation for 120 min, 58.2% of RhB can be removed by pure PbBiO2Cl. With the introduction of CNTs, the photocatalytic performance of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites greatly increased. The 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composite showed the highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency, 92.5% of RhB can be degraded after 120 min light illumination. When the amount of CNTs in CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composite was higher than 5 wt%, the photocatalytic activity would decrease. This phenomenon might be attributed to the excessive content of CNTs would impede the light absorption of PbBiO2Cl nanosheets. Similar results have been reported in previous studies [15,16,33,34]. Simultaneously, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of RhB over pure PbBiO2Cl and different CNTs/PbBiO2Cl samples were explored (Fig. 1 b). The variation of concentration over RhB versus reaction time accorded with the pseudo-first-order kinetic curves. The 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl displayed the highest photodegradation rate constant, about 1.1 times higher than pure PbBiO2Cl material.

Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) have been extensively used for curing bacterial infections. Due to could not be metabolized completely, a great deal of TC and CIP were discharged into natural water. Consequently, effective and thorough degradation of TC and CIP in the environment is of great significance [35]. Here, TC and CIP have been used for further exploring the photocatalytic activity of pure PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composite. In the dark reaction for 30 min, the equilibrium of adsorption and desorption was reached between the photocatalysts and the pollutants (Figs. S2a and S2b). As depicted in Fig. 2 a and c, 58.1% of TC and 64.8% of CIP can be photodegraded by pure PbBiO2Cl after irradiation for 6 h and 4 h, respectively. After the introduction of CNTs to the surface of PbBiO2Cl, 82.9% of TC and 78.5% of CIP can be degraded by 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl sample. In Fig. 2 b and d, the photocatalytic degradation kinetic curves of TC and CIP both followed the pseudo-first-order kinetic plots. The reaction rate constant for 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl was 2 times and 1.5 times higher than pure PbBiO2Cl for TC and CIP degradation. On account of the above experimental results, it can be found that CNTs act as a core role in the improvement of photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the cyclic stability of as-prepared photocatalyst was evaluated by photocatalytic degradation cycle experiments. It can be seen in Fig. S3, after 4 times cycle experiments, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl over RhB, TC and CIP all decreased slightly, which might attribute to the loss of 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl material during the cycle and pollutant molecules adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst cannot be completely removed. The results of photocatalytic degradation cycle experiments indicated that the as-prepared 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl material had good cycling stability.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis is a powerful method to analyze the crystalline phase and composition of the catalysts. In Fig. 3, it can be observed that the characteristic diffraction peaks of pure PbBiO2Cl and CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites belong to PbBiO2Cl (JCPDS No.13-0352) with orthorhombic phase structure. However, the typical diffraction peaks of CNTs (about 26°) can not be observed. This can be ascribed to the lower loading amount of CNTs in CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites. Similar results can also be obtained in other references [15,16].

The tight interface contact contributes to the effective separation of photogenerated electrons between CNTs and PbBiO2Cl. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image revealed the ultrathin nanosheet structure of pure PbBiO2Cl material ( Fig. 4 a). The corresponding High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images in Fig. 4 c indicated the lattice spacing was determined to be 0.28 nm accorded with (020) crystal plane of PbBiO2Cl material [17]. Furthermore, more CNTs could be fixed on the surface of PbBiO2Cl owing to the ionic liquid used to be reaction source. Therefore, CNTs were clearly observed on the surface of PbBiO2Cl nanosheets in Fig. 4 b, implying 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composite has been successfully prepared. Simultaneously, CNTs and PbBiO2Cl formed a close contact.

The chemical state and elemental composition of pure PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composite were determined by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique. As shown in the XPS survey spectra ( Fig. 5 a), the main elements in 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl were Bi, Pb, O, Cl and C elements, indicating that CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites were successfully synthesized. In the Bi 4f high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 5 b) of pure PbBiO2Cl, the peaks located at 159.0 eV and 164.4 eV belong to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, which demonstrated that Bi element existed in the valence state of Bi3+. The characteristic peaks centered at 138.3 eV and 143.1 eV corresponded Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2, respectively, impling Pb ions was in positive divalent (Fig. 5 c). Fig. 5 d displayed the high-resolution Cl 2p spectra of pure PbBiO2Cl, two peaks at 199.7 eV and 198.1 eV were assigned to Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2, indicating that the valance state of Cl was Cl−1. The O 1 s peaks located at 529.9 eV belong to the lattice oxygen in PbBiO2Cl material. Compared with pure PbBiO2Cl, the Bi 4f, Pb 4f, Cl 2p and O 1 s characteristic peak of 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composite all exhibited a slight shift towards the higher binding energy, which illustrated that the introduction of CNTs has a significant effect on the surface chemical environment of PbBiO2Cl nanosheets. Moreover, in the C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 5 f), peaks at binding energy of 284.8 eV was attributed to the C-C bond, which could account for the CNTs successfully modified on the surface of PbBiO2Cl nanosheets. The results of XPS analysis proved that the chemical environment on the surface of PbBiO2Cl was changed due to the strong interaction between CNTs and PbBiO2Cl [16,36].

It is generally accepted that the specific surface area will influence the photocatalytic performance. The specific surface area of pure PbBiO2Cl and CNTs/PbBiO2Cl have been studied employing BET analysis ( Fig. 6 and Fig. S4). The BET value of pure PbBiO2Cl is calculated to be 7.78 m2/g. With the introduction of CNTs, the BET of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites increase, the BET values of 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt% and 8 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl are calculated to be 11.09 m2/g, 17.20 m2/g, 25.85 m2/g and 31.58 m2/g, respectively. The higher BET value could provide sufficient adsorption sites to capture contaminants and reactive radicals, and further improve photocatalytic activity [6].

The optical properties of pure PbBiO2Cl and CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites have been measured by the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy ( Fig. 7 a). The onset absorption edge can be attributed to the photogenerated carriers of PbBiO2Cl via optical excitation. With the increasing loading amount of CNTs, the absorption edge of CNTs/PbBiO2Cl displays red shift. The enhanced light capture capability contributes to the generation of photogenerated carriers, thus increasing the photocatalytic performance. The energy bandgap value of PbBiO2Cl is estimated by the Kubelka-Munk function [37]:
αhν = A (hν – Eg)n/2
where α, h, ν, A and Eg stand for the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, a constant and band gap energy, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S5, the Eg value of PbBiO2Cl is calculated to be 2.48 eV.

The photoluminescence (PL) technique, electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) analysis and transient photocurrent are conducted to analyze the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in CNTs/PbBiO2Cl system. As depicted in Fig. 7 c, the PL spectra of PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl has an emission peak centered at 469 nm, and the peak intensity of 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl is low, which reveals that the introduction of CNTs could effectively accelerate the separation of photogenerated electrons [37]. As shown in Fig. 7 d, it can be observed that the photocurrent response value of 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl is higher than that of the single PbBiO2Cl. This experimental result demonstrates that CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites display a superior migration rate of photogenerated carriers [38]. CNTs can act as an efficient charge transporter so as to accelerate the separation of photogenerated carriers, thus enhance the photocatalytic efficiency [39]. Additionally, the radius of Nyquist circle for 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl is much smaller than that of single PbBiO2Cl, which indicates that CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites possess a nethermore resistance value (Fig. 7 b). The smaller arc radius represents the higher migration efficiency of interficial charge, which shows that the loading of CNTs on PbBiO2Cl nanosheets is beneficial to the faster transfer of photogenerated carriers [16]. The analysis of PL, EIS and transient photocurrent confirm that the efficient charge transfer contributes to the improved photocatalytic performance.

Radicals quenching experiments were conducted to identify the major active species in the degradation process. In Fig. 8, the photocatalytic performance of 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl is displayed with adding different radicals scavenger. When isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and t-butanol were added as •OH trapping agents, the photocatalytic performance can not be affected. However, when N2 (O2 •− inhibitor), disodium oxalate tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na: holes quenching agents) and ammonium oxalate (AO: holes quenching agents) were added, the photocatalytic performance can be inhibited obviously. The results of radicals quenching tests display that the holes and O2 •− play a vital part in the photocatalytic reactions, while •OH is not. The active species of free radicals were further confirmed by ESR technique ( Fig. 9). It can be observed that the characteristic peaks of O2 •− and •OH can not be detected in the darkness. Both PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl show the strong characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2 •− under visible light irradiation. And the characteristic peak intensity of 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl is stronger, which indicates that the introduction of CNTs can improve the molecular oxygen activation ability and generate more O2 •− [40]. Nevertheless, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-•OH cannot be observed for single PbBiO2Cl and 5 wt% CNTs/PbBiO2Cl. The ESR spin-trap tests demonstrate that O2 •− is the major species and •OH is not, which coincide with the radicals trapping experiments.

The valence band (VB) spectra was carried out to further explain the generation of radicals in this photocatalytic system. As shown in Fig. S6, the VB value of PbBiO2Cl is measured to be 1.55 eV. On account of the formula E
CB = E
VB – E
g , the conduction band (CB) value of PbBiO2Cl is calculated to −0.93 eV. The CB potential of PbBiO2Cl is more negative than E 0(O2/O2 •−) (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) [41], which indicates that the O2 adsorbed onto the surface of catalyst can be reduced to generate O2 •−. On the contrary, the VB value of PbBiO2Cl is lower than E 0(•OH/OH−) (2.38 eV vs. NHE) [42], signifying that the OH− can not be oxidized to generate •OH.

Given that the above characterizations and previous references [16,21], the photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. In Fig. 10, under visible light irradiation, the electrons on the VB of PbBiO2Cl can be motivated and migrate to the CB. Thanks to the tight interface contact of the two components and special conjugated π structure of CNTs, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of PbBiO2Cl will migrate rapidly to CNTs. The will lead to the effective separation of photogenerated carriers. Then the electrons on the CNTs will react with the adsorbed oxygen molecule to yield more O2 • −. Both holes and O2 • − can be involved into the degradation of organic contaminants. In this photocatalytic system, the loading of CNTs on PbBiO2Cl nanosheets ensures the sufficient interface contact, which enables the separation of photoexcited electrons. More photogenerated electron-hole pairs can be involved into the photocatalytic reactions, thus greatly improves the photocatalytic performance.

In this study, a variety of carbon nanotubes (CNTs)/PbBiO2Cl composites were successfully prepared via ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium chlorine ([C16mim]Cl) assisted hydrothermal process. The formation mechanism of the CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites on account of the preliminary formation of powerful coupling between the IL and CNTs and subsequent resulted in tight interface contact between CNTs and PbBiO2Cl. The results of steady-state fluorescence and photoelectrochemical characterizations indicated that CNTs with good electrical conductivity act as electron acceptors to accelerate the separation and transport of photocarriers, thus boosting the photodegrade performance. Compared to pure PbBiO2Cl, CNTs/PbBiO2Cl composites displayed greatly enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance over rhodamine B, tetracycline and ciprofloxacin under visible light irradiation. Free radical trapping experiments and electron spin resonance spectra analysis proved that both holes and superoxide radicals were identified as the major active species in the photocatalytic degradation process. Moreover, the tight junction of CNTs and PbBiO2Cl improved the molecular oxygen activation ability of PbBiO2Cl intrinsic material. Based on the above analysis, the possible photocatalytic mechanism has been proposed. Our findings suggest that carbon-based composite photocatalysts have potential applications in environmental remediation.

Zihan Liu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sheng Yin: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Qingsong Hu: Data curation, Investigation. Yi Ding: Data curation, Investigation. Jun Di: Data curation, Investigation. Jiexiang Xia: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision. Huaming Li: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21376109).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127894.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Designing 3D-MoS2 sponge as excellent cocatalysts in advanced oxidation processes for pollutant control
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.L. Zhu, J.H. Ji, J. Liu, S.Y. Mine, M. Matsuoka, J.L. Zhang, M.Y. Xing, Designing 3D-MoS2 sponge as excellent cocatalysts in advanced oxidation processes for pollutant control, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 59 (2020) 13968–13976.
